1: Then YHWH said to me, Though Moses and Samuel stood before me, yet my heart would not turn toward this people. Send them out of my sight, and let them go. YHWH's answer to the second lament-cycle (Jeremiah 14:19–22) is one of the most theologically sobering statements in all of Jeremiah. Moses and Samuel were the two great intercessors of Israel's history — Moses turned aside the golden-calf judgment (Exodus 32:11–14) and Samuel prayed at Mizpah (1 Samuel 7:5–11; Jeremiah 12:23). If even their intercession would be ineffective, no human prayer can reverse the coming judgment. The paradigm intercessors function here as the outer limit of what intercession can accomplish: even the best imaginable advocacy cannot obtain mercy for those whose covenant violation has crossed this threshold.
2: And when they ask you, 'Where shall we go?' you shall say to them, 'Thus says YHWH: Those who are for pestilence, to pestilence, and those who are for the sword, to the sword; those who are for famine, to famine, and those who are for captivity, to captivity'. The fourfold judgment triad expanded to include captivity. Each person will find their destined fate — the formula is almost mechanical, reflecting the precision of covenant-curse fulfillment. The people's question (where shall we go?) is existentially profound: when every direction means death or exile, there is nowhere to flee. The answer is not comfort but assignment.
3: I will appoint over them four kinds of destroyers, declares YHWH: the sword to kill, the dogs to tear, and the birds of the air and the beasts of the earth to devour and destroy. The four destroyers elaborate the judgment: sword (military death), dogs (scavenging corpses in the streets — deeply dishonorable in the ANE, cf. 1 Kings 21:23–24 on Jezebel), birds of the air, and beasts. The image of unburied bodies consumed by dogs and birds is one of the most severe covenant-curse formulations in Deuteronomy 28:26 and appears repeatedly in the judgment oracles against kings (1 Kings 14:11; Jeremiah 16:4).
4: And I will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth because of what Manasseh the son of Hezekiah, king of Judah, did in Jerusalem. The specific naming of Manasseh is historically precise and theologically significant. Manasseh's 55-year reign (c. 697–642 BC) was the longest of any Judahite king and was characterized by the worst apostasy — he reversed Hezekiah's reforms, reinstated Baal altars, made his son pass through fire, practiced divination, and filled Jerusalem with innocent blood (2 Kings 21:1–16). The Deuteronomistic History explicitly identifies Manasseh's sins as the cause of the Babylonian exile (2 Kings 24:3–4). Even Josiah's reforms could not undo the generational damage.
5: Who will have pity on you, O Jerusalem, or who will grieve for you? Who will turn aside to ask about your welfare? The rhetorical questions function as a lament over a city already dead. The Hebrew menud (who will grieve) and yanud (who will turn aside) describe the gestures of condolence — nodding the head in sympathy, pausing to inquire. When no one performs these gestures, it signals total social abandonment. Jerusalem has lost the compassion of her neighbors, just as she lost the compassion of YHWH (v1). The city has been deserted on every side.
6: You have rejected me, declares YHWH; you keep going backward. So I have stretched out my hand against you and destroyed you — I am weary of relenting. I am weary of relenting (nileti hinachem) — one of the most striking divine self-descriptions in the Hebrew Bible. YHWH has repeatedly relented from judgment in response to prophetic intercession and national repentance (cf. Amos 7:1–6 where YHWH relents twice). But this relenting has been exploited — the people reformed briefly and then returned to apostasy. The exhaustion of divine patience is not a failure of divine love but the consequence of repeated manipulation of covenant mercy.
7: I have winnowed them with a winnowing fork in the gates of the land; I have bereaved them; I have destroyed my people; they did not turn from their ways. Winnowing — the process of throwing threshed grain into the air so the wind separates wheat from chaff — is used as a judgment image (cf. Psalms 1:4; Isaiah 41:16; Jeremiah 4:11). The gates of the land are the entry-points of the country — the places where people and trade flow. The judgment has already begun; YHWH has been sifting the nation. They did not turn from their ways: the purpose of earlier judgments was to produce repentance; they failed to do so.
8: I have made their widows more in number than the sand of the sea; I have brought against the mothers of young men a destroyer at noonday; I have made anguish and terror fall upon her suddenly. The accumulation of widows — women bereaved of husbands by military death — is measured against the sand metaphor (normally used for Abraham's innumerable descendants, Genesis 22:17). The reversal is stark: where the promise was innumerable descendants, now the result is innumerable bereaved survivors. At noonday: ancient attacks typically came at dawn or dusk; a noonday assault removes even the comfort of predictable timing. The terror is total and sudden.
9: She who bore seven has grown faint; she has fainted away; her sun went down while it was yet day; she has been shamed and disgraced. And the rest of them I will give to the sword before their enemies, declares YHWH. She who bore seven — having seven sons was the pinnacle of maternal fulfillment in Israelite culture (cf. Ruth 4:15; 1 Samuel 2:5). The woman with seven sons represents the most blessed of mothers. Even she will lose everything. Her sun went down while it was yet day: the image of untimely death — the sun that should have shone through a full day setting at noon. The contrast between expected flourishing and actual devastation could not be sharper.
10: Woe is me, my mother, that you bore me, a man of strife and contention to the whole land! I have not lent, nor have I borrowed, yet all of them curse me. Jeremiah's confession (the Confessions of Jeremiah — Jeremiah 11:18–23; Jeremiah 12:1–6; Jeremiah 15:10–21; Jeremiah 17:14–18; Jeremiah 18:19–23; Jeremiah 20:7–18) begins here in full force. The prophet wishes he had never been born — a sentiment that appears in Job 3 and reaches its NT echo in the cry of dereliction. The complaint that he is cursed by everyone despite personal financial integrity highlights the social isolation of the prophet's role: the messenger bearing unwelcome news is held responsible for the news itself.
11: YHWH said: Have I not set you free for their good? Have I not pleaded for you before the enemy in the time of trouble and in the time of distress? YHWH's response to Jeremiah's lament is a reminder of divine protection. The verse is difficult to translate but the thrust is reassurance: YHWH has been and will be Jeremiah's advocate. The prophet's suffering does not occur because YHWH has abandoned him but within a larger purpose — his ministry will ultimately benefit those who now oppose him. This is covenant protection of the covenant messenger.
12: Can iron break iron from the north, and bronze? The rhetorical question invokes the metallurgical metaphor: the iron and bronze from the north (Babylon and its weapons) cannot be broken by ordinary weapons. The coming enemy is stronger than anything Judah can resist. The only option is not military resistance but submission to the covenant judgment YHWH is directing. This connects to Jeremiah's later counsel to surrender to Babylon (Jeremiah 27:8–11) as the theologically appropriate response to YHWH's judgment-through-Nebuchadnezzar.
13: Your wealth and your treasures I will give as spoil, without price, for all your sins, throughout all your territory. The confiscation of Judah's accumulated wealth by Babylon is framed as covenant confiscation — YHWH is taking back what he gave, because the covenant conditions for possession have been violated. Without price means the wealth will be given away cheaply, treated as worthless plunder rather than valued treasure. The reversal of the promised blessing of material abundance (Deuteronomy 28:3–8) is total.
14: I will make you serve your enemies in a land that you do not know, for in my anger a fire is kindled that shall burn forever. Exile service under foreign masters is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:47–48 — the failure to serve YHWH with joy and gladness results in serving enemies in deprivation. A fire that shall burn forever (esh qadechah ad-olam tuqad): the hyperbolic permanence of divine wrath communicates the severity of the judgment, not necessarily its eternal duration — olam in the Hebrew Bible frequently means an indefinitely long period rather than strict eternity.
15: O YHWH, you know; remember me and visit me, and take vengeance for me on my persecutors. In your forbearance take me not away; know that for your sake I bear reproach. Jeremiah's petition pivots from complaint to covenant appeal. You know (attah yadeta) — not merely cognitive knowledge but YHWH's intimate, caring attention. Remember me and visit me: the covenant language of divine attention (cf. Genesis 8:1 — God remembered Noah; Genesis 21:1 — YHWH visited Sarah). Jeremiah asks to be remembered before he is consumed by his persecutors. For your sake I bear reproach: the prophet's suffering is not personal but vocational — he is shamed because he serves YHWH.
16: Your words were found, and I ate them, and your words became to me a joy and the delight of my heart, for I am called by your name, O YHWH, God of hosts. One of the most beautiful verses in Jeremiah. The eating of the word recalls Ezekiel 2–3 (Ezekiel literally eats the scroll) and anticipates Revelation 10:9–10 (John eats the scroll — sweet in the mouth, bitter in the stomach). For Jeremiah the word of YHWH was initially pure joy — the delight of divine communication, the honor of bearing YHWH's name. The tension with vv10, 17–18 is intentional: the prophet's calling began in joy and has descended into isolation.
17: I did not sit in the company of merrymakers, nor did I rejoice; I sat alone, because your hand was upon me, for you had filled me with indignation. The social isolation of Jeremiah's prophetic calling is stated plainly. He could not participate in normal communal celebration because the word he carried — impending doom — made celebration impossible. Your hand was upon me: the prophetic compulsion that Jeremiah cannot evade (cf. Jeremiah 20:7–9 — the word burning in his bones). Filled me with indignation: YHWH's own moral outrage at Judah's apostasy has been communicated to and taken up by the prophet, making normal social participation impossible.
18: Why is my pain unceasing, my wound incurable, refusing to be healed? Will you be to me like a deceitful brook, like waters that fail? The most daring of Jeremiah's accusations against YHWH. The deceitful brook (nachal akhzav) — the wadis of Palestine flow in winter from rainfall and run dry in summer precisely when most needed. YHWH has seemed to Jeremiah like a wadi that offered water but dried up at the moment of greatest need. The accusation verges on blasphemy by the standards of conventional piety — but it is the lament tradition at its most honest. The Psalms of lament (Ps 22, 88) use similar language of divine abandonment.
19: Therefore thus says YHWH: If you return, I will restore you, and you shall stand before me. If you utter what is precious, and not what is worthless, you shall be as my mouth. They shall turn to you, but you shall not turn to them. YHWH's response challenges Jeremiah's own spiritual state. The prophet who has been accusing YHWH of being like a failing wadi needs to return himself — the call to return (teshuvah) applies to the prophet, not just the people. Utter what is precious, not what is worthless: distinguish between the authentic divine word and the complaints that sound like the false prophets. The final reversal — they shall turn to you, not you to them — is the proper posture of the prophet vis-à-vis the people.
20: And I will make you to this people a fortified wall of bronze; they will fight against you, but they shall not prevail over you, for I am with you to save you and deliver you, declares YHWH. The renewal of Jeremiah's commission echoes Jeremiah 1:18–19 almost verbatim: I have made you this day a fortified city, an iron pillar, and bronze walls. The bronze wall is not personal invulnerability — Jeremiah will suffer imprisonment, physical abuse, and near-death — but rather that his prophetic mission will not be ultimately defeated. The enemies who fight against him will not prevail because YHWH is his defender.
21: I will deliver you out of the hand of the wicked and redeem you from the grasp of the ruthless. The covenant rescue language (padah, redeem; natsal, deliver) closes the divine response. YHWH is promising covenant protection for his covenant messenger — not escape from suffering but ultimate vindication. The fulfilled form of this promise is visible in Jeremiah's rescue from the cistern (Jeremiah 38:6–13), his protection by Ebed-melech, and his survival of Jerusalem's fall when others perished.
